diary.title: Investment journal
diary.subtitle: Capture hypotheses, risks, and criteria — no trading recommendations
diary.subtitleGlossary: Consider {diversification} and {volatility} before acting on impulse.

diary.selectEntryHint: Select an entry on the left to see retrospective and details.
diary.fieldActionHint: Not advice — your plan for yourself
diary.fieldHorizonHint: When you plan to revisit the idea
diary.fieldConfidenceHint: Subjective score, not a return forecast

portfolio.title: Portfolio
portfolio.subtitle: Manual positions and structure review — not investment advice
portfolio.subtitleGlossary: Review {diversification} and concentration — more than short-term P/L.
portfolio.empty: No positions yet. Add your first from the catalog or by ticker.
portfolio.deleteConfirm: Remove this position from the portfolio?
portfolio.fieldGoalHint: Why you hold this position — for yourself, not a recommendation

hint.lowDiversification: You have few positions. Consider whether you rely too heavily on a small set of ideas.
hint.highSymbolConcentration: {symbol} is about {weight}% of the portfolio. High concentration can amplify volatility.
hint.highEquityWeight: Shares are about {weight}% of the portfolio. This structure may be more sensitive to market drawdowns.
hint.highCurrencyConcentration: About {weight}% of the portfolio is tied to one currency zone. Currency risk may matter.
hint.highCurrencyConcentrationGlossary: About {weight}% in one currency zone. Consider {volatility} and FX exposure.
hint.partialPriceData: Some positions lack live quotes — totals may be incomplete.

portfolio.disclaimer: Data is entered manually. Structure notes are educational and not investment advice.

bonds.title: Bond helper
bonds.subtitle: MOEX bond catalog and educational review — not investment advice
bonds.introSubtitle: Short notes before diving into a specific issue
bonds.introWhatIsBondBody: A bond is debt: you lend money to an issuer and typically receive {coupon}s. Face value is repaid at maturity if the issuer honors obligations. Market price can move — it is not a guaranteed deposit.
bonds.introHighYield: Why high yield ≠ “better than a deposit”
bonds.introHighYieldBody: Higher yield often prices in more risk: maturity, liquidity, issuer credit. Comparing to bank deposits without default and price risk disclaimers is misleading.
bonds.introRateRiskBody: When rate expectations rise, older lower-coupon issues may fall in price. Longer maturities are usually more rate-sensitive.
bonds.disclaimer: Educational context from market data. Not investment advice.
bonds.glossaryHint: Context: {coupon}, {yield}, {issuer}, and {faceValue} on this bond card.

watchlist.title: Watchlist
watchlist.subtitle: Track assets and record your research status
watchlist.subtitleGlossary: A watchlist is about {diversification} and patience — not rushing to buy.
watchlist.empty: Watchlist is empty. Add assets from the catalog.

errors.errorDescription: We could not load this page. Please try again.
errors.notFoundDescription: The address does not exist or has been moved.

landing.heroTitle: Get the market first — decide later
landing.heroSubtitle: Plain talk on the market, risks, and an idea journal. AI helps pull context for names and entries — no buy/sell hype, no FOMO.
landing.heroNote: We don’t drop “just buy it” takes. We help you think out loud: ask better questions and log hypotheses until things click.
landing.directionsSubtitle: Pick a lane — we’ll match the tone of tips and explanations to your goal.

d1.title: Get into investing
d1.body: Basics without the lecture vibe: stocks, bonds, risks — with Russian market examples, not a dusty textbook.

d2.title: See what the market’s doing
d2.body: Today’s picture, sectors, and stand-out moves — without terminal chaos or feed panic.

d3.title: Build a watchlist
d3.body: 5–7 names on your list, 2–4 weeks of watching how they behave — no FOMO “buy now” energy.

d4.title: Run an idea journal
d4.body: Log thesis, risks, and criteria — then honestly compare what you expected vs what happened.

d5.title: Sanity-check your portfolio
d5.body: Where you’re overweight, where you’re concentrated, and what to ask each line — no “guaranteed x’s”.

d6.title: Get bonds, for real
d6.body: Issuer, maturity, yield, and risks — with caveats, not the “it’s like a deposit but cooler” myth.

landing.featuresSubtitle: On every asset card — six questions plus AI hints for each: from “what is it?” to “ready to act?” — not a ready-made trade.

q1.title: What is this instrument?
q1.body: Human words: a stock is a slice of a company, a bond is debt, a fund is a basket. Where returns might come from and why price moves at all.

q2.title: What is happening now?
q2.body: Day, week, and month moves and how the name looks vs the market. Not just candles — the story, not numbers for a screenshot.

q3.title: Why might that be?
q3.body: AI suggests “maybe because…” angles — rates, FX, news, sector. Likely and possible, never “we already know the reason.”

q4.title: What are the risks?
q4.body: Market, sector, issuer, liquidity, rules. So you see where it could hurt, not only the upside reel.

q5.title: Does it fit me?
q5.body: Your horizon, goals, and how much drawdown you can stomach. In the journal — your thesis and “nailed it / missed it” criteria, not someone else’s voice.

q6.title: What to check before acting?
q6.body: Mini checklist: what confirms the idea, what breaks it, what you’ll watch. A frame for your call — not a ready-made “enter now” button.

diaryCycle.intro: Not messy notes — an actual loop:

landing.diaryBody: We don’t say “don’t.” AI can flag weak spots in your thesis and questions for retro — the call and responsibility stay yours.

diaryPoints.p1: Thesis: why the name is on your radar
diaryPoints.p3: Criteria: when you nailed it vs whiffed
diaryPoints.p4: Retro: what changed in your head

landing.howSubtitle: Three steps — from chill observation to a decision you won’t cringe at later.

s1.title: Sign up + level
s1.body: Email, password, and your experience level — so AI and hints don’t sound like a bank brochure if you’re just getting started.

s2.title: Your watchlist
s2.body: Add MOEX names from the catalog and track moves — no “hurry before the close” pressure.

s3.title: Thesis in the journal
s3.body: Log the thought, risks, and criteria — AI can help sharpen the wording; then come back and compare with reality.

landing.ctaSubtitle: Free sign-up: catalog, watchlist, and journal in one place — no extra noise.

auth.passwordTooWeakHint: Strengthen your password to fair or strong level
auth.oauthError: External sign-in failed. Try email and password.

advanced.title: Experienced investor
advanced.description: Keeps a journal and reviews decisions

overview.title: Market picture
overview.subtitle: What is happening on MOEX today — indices, sectors, and stand-out moves. Not investment advice.
overview.errorDescription: Check the API connection and refresh the page.
overview.fxSubtitle: Exchange TOM quotes: RUB per 1 USD/EUR/CNY; RUB/USD is a reference inverse.
overview.plainLanguageBody: Numbers on screen are not a call to trade. They help you read market mood: who is stronger today, where volatility is higher, and what context matters for your ideas.
overview.plainLanguageIndexHint: Comparing a name to an {index} is context, not a signal. A {sector} shows how the industry behaves as a whole.
overview.disclaimer: Summary based on MOEX market data. Not investment advice.
overview.beginnerCardBody: If you just opened a brokerage account, start with a short guide: goal, watchlist, and a journal — without rushing to buy.

up.title: Market looks moderately positive
up.body: IMOEX is up for the session. That is not a guarantee the trend continues — rates, FX, and news can change the picture quickly.

down.title: Market is under pressure
down.body: IMOEX is down for the session. A falling index does not mean “everything is broken forever” — your horizon and idea criteria matter more.

flat.title: No clear market direction
flat.body: IMOEX is almost flat for the session. On days like this, sectors and single names tell more than hunting for one headline.

learn.title: Learn
learn.subtitle: Investing basics, market instruments, and common mistakes — Russian market context, no buy/sell calls.
learn.risksCardDescription: Market, rate, FX, and more — with concrete situations.
learn.glossaryCardDescription: Terms in plain language — same as in-app tooltips.
learn.disclaimer: Educational content only. Not investment advice.

getting-started.title: Where to start on the market
getting-started.summary: Observation and questions first — not a rush to trade.

s1.title: Step 1: goal and horizon
s1.body: Write why you are here: safety buffer, a 3–5 year goal, or a small learning amount. Without a horizon, any drawdown feels catastrophic.

s2.title: Step 2: watchlist
s2.body: Add 5–7 names and watch them vs the index for 2–4 weeks. That is cheaper than learning from an impulse buy.

s3.title: Step 3: idea journal
s3.body: When you think “I should buy”, draft a hypothesis: why, risks, success and failure criteria. The platform helps sharpen the wording.

stocks-and-bonds.title: Stocks vs bonds
stocks-and-bonds.summary: Equity slice vs lender — different risks.

s1.title: Stock
s1.body: A stock is a share of a company. Return may come from price growth and dividends; price can fall. Dividends are not guaranteed.

s2.title: Bond
s2.body: A bond is debt: coupons and par at maturity if the issuer performs. Market price changes — not a fixed deposit.

s3.title: Why both matter
s3.body: Stocks are usually more volatile; bonds can smooth the picture but have rate and credit risk. Mix depends on horizon and drawdown tolerance.

s4.title: Questions to ask
s4.body: “Can I handle a 20–30% equity drawdown?” and “Why is this bond’s yield so high?” are different but equally important.

s1.title: Emotional entries
s1.body: A weekly rally, a social post, or “everyone buys” are weak reasons. Draft a hypothesis and list risks first.

s2.title: Deposit comparison without caveats
s2.body: “18% bond vs bank” ignores default, price risk, and liquidity. Higher yield often means higher risk.

s3.title: One name = the market
s3.body: Concentration in one issuer or sector amplifies one headline. Watch weights in watchlist and portfolio.

s4.title: No failure criteria
s4.body: Without “when is the idea wrong?” it is hard to learn. The journal is for writing that down early.

instrument-stocks.title: Stocks on MOEX
instrument-stocks.summary: What to read on the card and in the journal.

s1.title: Price and context
s1.body: A daily move is not cheap/expensive. Compare to index, sector, and your horizon.

s2.title: Sector and news
s2.body: Banks, energy, IT react to different drivers. Use possible factors, not facts.

s3.title: Dividends
s3.body: Not guaranteed. Check policy and business headroom.

s1.title: Five questions
s1.body: Issuer? Maturity? Coupon? Why this yield? How liquid is the issue?

s2.title: Sovereign vs corporate
s2.body: Government and corporate paper carry different credit stories.

s3.title: Rates and price
s3.body: Rising rate expectations can hit older low-coupon issues. Longer maturity = more sensitivity.

s1.title: Holdings
s1.body: Read what is inside — that is your real risk.

s2.title: Fees and tracking
s2.body: Funds charge fees; tracking error happens. Compare with buying components if you want depth.

s3.title: When it fits
s3.body: Often used for diversification, but drawdowns still happen.

scenario-watchlist.title: Scenario: first watchlist
scenario-watchlist.summary: 5–7 names and a month of observation.

s1.title: Day 1
s1.body: Pick different sectors, add to watchlist as “Watching”.

s2.title: Weeks 1–2
s2.body: Use market picture and cards; write questions, not trades.

s3.title: Weeks 3–4
s3.body: Mark 1–2 names “Researching”. Draft a journal entry if an idea appears.

s4.title: Outcome
s4.body: Goal is context habit, not monthly profit. Compare notes to the index.

scenario-checklist.title: Scenario: pre-idea checklist
scenario-checklist.summary: Questions for yourself, not the market.

s1.title: Horizon
s1.body: Does the instrument match your timeline?

s2.title: Risks
s2.body: Name at least two: market-wide and issuer/sector specific.

s3.title: Criteria
s3.body: What proves the idea right — and what proves it wrong? Log in the journal.

glossary.title: Knowledge base
glossary.subtitle: Terms you meet in the catalog, bonds, and portfolio. Underlined words in the UI show the same hints.
glossary.disclaimer: Definitions are simplified for learning, not legal or tax advice.

stock.title: Stock
stock.body: A share of a listed company. Return may come from price and dividends; drawdowns are normal.

bond.title: Bond
bond.body: Debt security: coupons and par at maturity if the issuer performs.

dividend.title: Dividend
dividend.body: Possible profit distribution to shareholders — not guaranteed.

coupon.title: Coupon
coupon.body: Periodic bond payment; size and schedule depend on issue terms.

index.title: Index
index.body: A basket benchmark (e.g. IMOEX) to compare a name with the broad market.

diversification.title: Diversification
diversification.body: Spreading across issuers, sectors, and asset types so one story does not dominate.

volatility.title: Volatility
volatility.body: How much and how fast price moves.

issuer.title: Issuer
issuer.body: Who issued the security — credit risk sits here.

yield.title: Yield
yield.body: Expected return given price, coupon, and maturity. High yield often reflects higher risk.

lot.title: Lot
lot.body: Minimum trade size on the exchange.

sector.title: Sector
sector.body: Industry group — helps see shared drivers, not only one ticker.

face-value.title: Face value
face-value.body: Par repaid at maturity if terms hold. Market price can differ.

risksSection.title: Risks
risksSection.subtitle: Situations to imagine before you decide — no forecasts, no buy/sell.

market.title: Market risk
market.description: The whole market can move — even a solid company is not isolated.
market.example: Index falls on geopolitics — your stock may drop too though fundamentals look fine.

rate.title: Rate risk
rate.description: Policy rate expectations hit bonds, credit costs, and equity valuations.
rate.example: Higher rate expectations pressure long bonds and debt-heavy equities.

currency.title: FX risk
currency.description: Ruble moves affect importers, exporters, and FX-linked balance sheets.
currency.example: Ruble strength can hurt exporters; weakness can hit importers.

dividend.title: Dividend risk
dividend.description: Payouts can be cut or skipped — markets often price that early.
dividend.example: Company keeps cash for growth — dividend below expectations, price may adjust.

liquidity.title: Liquidity risk
liquidity.description: You may not trade quickly near the quoted price.
liquidity.example: Thin bond: wide spread, urgent sale can be costly vs the screen price.

aiAssistant.title: Educational assistant
aiAssistant.subtitle: Ask about instruments, risks, and terms — no buy/sell/hold advice.
aiAssistant.placeholder: E.g. why are bonds sensitive to interest rates?
aiAssistant.errorGeneric: Could not get an answer. Try again later.

examples.rates: Why do rising rates affect bonds?
examples.imoex: Why compare a stock to the IMOEX index?
examples.bondsVsDeposit: How is a bond different from a deposit in terms of risk?
